threadTitle: Things that instantly tell you someone just moved here
body: You can always tell the new people because they treat the coffee queue like a networking opportunity. The regulars are trying to look half-dead in peace.
I heard a guy on the N63 say 'we don't need to talk this out, we need to get home' and that felt like the most adult sentence I've heard all month.
My rule is that if a place still lets people stand outside holding one beer for forty minutes without making it an identity, it hasn't fully died yet.

threadTitle: Most berlin thing that happened to you this week
body: Woman at the post office corrected my article, switched to English out of pity, then looked annoyed that I answered in English. Extremely efficient humiliation.

threadTitle: Why every cafe feels the same now
body: The weird thing about coffee places here is that they all want to look casual while charging you enough to imply ambition. Even the stools feel venture-backed.
Tourists stop in the middle of the sidewalk to decide if a burrito line means authenticity. Locals just clock the speed, count the construction vests, and commit.

threadTitle: How you know a brunch place is not for locals
body: The real sign is not the menu, it's whether anyone inside looks remotely rushed. If every table is performing leisure, the prices already know who they are for.
There is a bar where the same man starts arguing about football before noon and somehow that makes the whole square feel less temporary.
